categories.name: Complete Blood Count (CBC)
categories.description: Evaluates overall health and detects conditions like anemia, infection, and clotting issues.

Hemoglobin.description: A protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen throughout the body.
Hemoglobin.low_explanation: Low hemoglobin (anemia) means your body's tissues are not receiving enough oxygen, which can cause fatigue, weakness, pale skin, and shortness of breath.
Hemoglobin.high_explanation: High hemoglobin (erythrocytosis) can occur due to dehydration, smoking, living at high altitudes, or chronic lung conditions.

Red Blood Cell (RBC).description: Cells that carry oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body.
Red Blood Cell (RBC).low_explanation: Low RBC count is typical of anemia, nutritional deficiencies (iron, B12, folate), or bone marrow disorders.
Red Blood Cell (RBC).high_explanation: High RBC count can indicate dehydration, congenital heart disease, sleep apnea, or smoking.

White Blood Cell (WBC).description: Cells that protect the body against infectious diseases and foreign invaders.
White Blood Cell (WBC).low_explanation: Low WBC count (leukopenia) makes you more susceptible to infections. Can be caused by bone marrow issues, autoimmune disorders, or viral infections.
White Blood Cell (WBC).high_explanation: High WBC count (leukocytosis) usually indicates an active infection, inflammation, stress, or immune response.

Platelet Count.description: Small cell fragments involved in blood clotting and wound healing.
Platelet Count.low_explanation: Low platelets (thrombocytopenia) can cause easy bruising and prolonged bleeding. Can be triggered by immune responses, viral infections, or medications.
Platelet Count.high_explanation: High platelets (thrombocytosis) can lead to abnormal blood clots. Can be caused by inflammation, iron deficiency, or bone marrow disorders.

Hematocrit.description: The proportion of red blood cells to the total volume of blood.
Hematocrit.low_explanation: Low hematocrit is another indicator of anemia or overhydration.
Hematocrit.high_explanation: High hematocrit indicates dehydration or conditions like polycythemia.

categories.name: Liver Function Test (LFT)
categories.description: Measures enzymes, proteins, and bilirubin to assess how well the liver is functioning.

SGPT (ALT).description: Alanine aminotransferase — an enzyme found mostly in liver cells.
SGPT (ALT).low_explanation: Low ALT levels are generally normal and expected.
SGPT (ALT).high_explanation: Elevated ALT is a strong indicator of liver injury. Common causes include fatty liver disease, alcohol use, viral hepatitis, or medications like acetaminophen.

SGOT (AST).description: Aspartate aminotransferase — found in the liver, heart, and muscles.
SGOT (AST).low_explanation: Low AST is normal and healthy.
SGOT (AST).high_explanation: Elevated AST suggests liver injury, muscle damage, or cardiac stress. When elevated with ALT, it strongly points to liver issues.

Total Bilirubin.description: A byproduct of red blood cell breakdown processed by the liver.
Total Bilirubin.low_explanation: Low bilirubin is generally not a cause for concern.
Total Bilirubin.high_explanation: High bilirubin can cause jaundice (yellowing of skin/eyes). Can indicate liver congestion, bile duct blockage, or Gilbert's syndrome.

Alkaline Phosphatase (ALP).description: An enzyme related to the bile ducts, gallbladder, and bones.
Alkaline Phosphatase (ALP).low_explanation: Low ALP is rare but can indicate malnutrition or zinc deficiency.
Alkaline Phosphatase (ALP).high_explanation: High ALP suggests bile duct obstruction, liver disease, or bone conditions.

Albumin.description: The main protein made by the liver that keeps fluid from leaking out of blood vessels.
Albumin.low_explanation: Low albumin can indicate liver disease, kidney disease, malnutrition, or chronic inflammation.
Albumin.high_explanation: High albumin is almost always caused by dehydration.

categories.name: Kidney Function Test (KFT)
categories.description: Evaluates how well the kidneys are filtering waste products from the blood.

Serum Creatinine.description: A waste product from muscle metabolism filtered out by the kidneys.
Serum Creatinine.low_explanation: Low creatinine may indicate low muscle mass or malnutrition.
Serum Creatinine.high_explanation: High creatinine indicates reduced kidney filtering capacity (reduced GFR). Can result from dehydration, kidney disease, or high protein intake.

Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN).description: Measures the amount of nitrogen in the blood that comes from urea, a waste product of protein metabolism.
Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN).low_explanation: Low BUN may indicate malnutrition, liver disease, or overhydration.
Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN).high_explanation: High BUN suggests decreased kidney function, dehydration, high protein intake, or GI bleeding. Often interpreted alongside creatinine (BUN/Creatinine ratio).

Uric Acid.description: A chemical produced when the body breaks down purines found in certain foods and drinks.
Uric Acid.low_explanation: Low uric acid is generally benign but can occasionally be seen with certain medications or rare genetic conditions.
Uric Acid.high_explanation: High uric acid (hyperuricemia) can lead to gout (painful joint inflammation) or kidney stones. Often associated with a high-purine diet, alcohol consumption, or kidney disease.

eGFR.description: Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate — the most accurate measure of kidney function.
eGFR.low_explanation: Low eGFR indicates chronic kidney disease (CKD). Stages: 45-59 (mild-moderate), 30-44 (moderate-severe), <30 (severe). Requires urgent medical follow-up.
eGFR.high_explanation: eGFR above 60 is generally considered normal. Very high values can sometimes occur in early diabetes mellitus (hyperfiltration).

Sodium (Na+).description: An electrolyte that controls fluid balance and nerve/muscle function.
Sodium (Na+).low_explanation: Low sodium (hyponatremia) can cause confusion, fatigue, muscle cramps, and in severe cases, seizures. Often from excess water intake, kidney issues, or heart failure.
Sodium (Na+).high_explanation: High sodium (hypernatremia) can cause intense thirst, confusion, and lethargy. Usually from dehydration or inability to access water.

Potassium (K+).description: An electrolyte essential for heart and muscle function.
Potassium (K+).low_explanation: Low potassium (hypokalemia) can cause muscle weakness, leg cramps, and dangerous heart arrhythmias. Often from vomiting, diarrhea, or diuretic medications.
Potassium (K+).high_explanation: High potassium (hyperkalemia) can also cause dangerous heart rhythm abnormalities. Often from kidney disease, ACE inhibitor medications, or excessive supplementation.

categories.name: Thyroid Profile
categories.description: Assesses thyroid gland function which controls metabolism, energy, and many vital body functions.

TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone).description: Produced by the pituitary gland to stimulate the thyroid to produce hormones. The most sensitive test for thyroid disorders.
TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone).low_explanation: Low TSH indicates the thyroid is overactive (hyperthyroidism) — producing too much hormone. Symptoms: rapid heartbeat, weight loss, anxiety, heat intolerance, tremors.
TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone).high_explanation: High TSH indicates the thyroid is underactive (hypothyroidism) — not producing enough hormone. Symptoms: fatigue, weight gain, constipation, cold intolerance, depression.

Free T4 (Thyroxine).description: The main hormone produced and released by the thyroid gland.
Free T4 (Thyroxine).low_explanation: Low Free T4 confirms hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid). Usually treated with daily levothyroxine (synthetic T4) tablets.
Free T4 (Thyroxine).high_explanation: High Free T4 confirms hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid). Can be caused by Graves' disease, toxic nodular goiter, or excess iodine intake.

Free T3 (Triiodothyronine).description: The active form of thyroid hormone that directly affects metabolism in cells.
Free T3 (Triiodothyronine).low_explanation: Low Free T3 is associated with hypothyroidism or 'low T3 syndrome' seen during severe illness or starvation.
Free T3 (Triiodothyronine).high_explanation: High Free T3 is associated with hyperthyroidism, particularly T3-toxicosis (where only T3 is elevated while T4 is normal).

categories.name: Lipid Profile
categories.description: Measures cholesterol and triglycerides to assess cardiovascular (heart disease) risk.

Total Cholesterol.description: Total amount of cholesterol in the blood. Includes both LDL (bad) and HDL (good) cholesterol.
Total Cholesterol.low_explanation: Very low cholesterol (<150 mg/dL) may indicate malnutrition, hyperthyroidism, or liver disease, though it is rare.
Total Cholesterol.high_explanation: High total cholesterol (>200 mg/dL) raises risk of atherosclerosis (arterial plaque), heart attack, and stroke. Diet, exercise, and statins are the main interventions.

LDL Cholesterol.description: Low-Density Lipoprotein — known as 'bad' cholesterol that deposits on artery walls.
LDL Cholesterol.low_explanation: Very low LDL is generally beneficial. Some statins and PCSK9 inhibitors intentionally drive LDL very low for heart disease prevention.
LDL Cholesterol.high_explanation: High LDL (>130 mg/dL, or >70 for heart disease patients) significantly raises cardiovascular risk. Reduce via a low-saturated-fat diet, regular exercise, and statins if needed.

HDL Cholesterol.description: High-Density Lipoprotein — known as 'good' cholesterol that helps remove LDL from arteries.
HDL Cholesterol.low_explanation: Low HDL (<40 mg/dL) is a major independent risk factor for heart disease. Associated with smoking, obesity, inactivity, and a high-carb diet.
HDL Cholesterol.high_explanation: High HDL (>60 mg/dL) is cardioprotective and is considered a negative risk factor for heart disease. Exercise is the best way to raise HDL.

Triglycerides.description: A type of fat (lipid) found in the blood. High levels are a risk factor for pancreatitis and cardiovascular disease.
Triglycerides.low_explanation: Low triglycerides are generally not a concern.
Triglycerides.high_explanation: High triglycerides (>150 mg/dL) are associated with metabolic syndrome, obesity, diabetes, excessive alcohol consumption, and high-carb diet. Very high levels (>500) can cause acute pancreatitis.
